mission.title: Install llm-d Intelligent Inference Scheduling
mission.description: Deploy the recommended out-of-the-box scheduling configuration for vLLM deployments using llm-d. This well-lit path reduces tail latency and increases throughput through load-aware and approximate prefix-cache-aware balancing. Defaults to 16 GPUs (8 replicas x 2 GPUs each) but supports fewer accelerators, CPU-only mode, and multiple hardware backends (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel XPU, Intel Gaudi, TPU).

steps.title: Install prerequisite client tools
steps.description:
Ensure you have the required client tools installed:
- `helm` (v3+)
- `helmfile` (v0.169+)
- `kubectl` (v1.29+)
- `yq` (v4+)

See the [client setup guide](https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/prereq/client-setup) for detailed instructions.

steps.title: Configure Gateway API and infrastructure
steps.description:
Ensure your cluster has:
1. Sufficient GPU resources (16 GPUs default, or fewer with modified values)
2. Gateway API CRDs installed
3. A gateway provider deployed (Istio is the default)
4. The monitoring stack installed

See the [infrastructure prerequisites](https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/prereq/infrastructure) and [gateway provider guide](https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/prereq/gateway-provider).

steps.title: Create namespace and HuggingFace token secret
steps.description:
Create a namespace for the deployment and add your HuggingFace token:
```bash
export NAMESPACE=llm-d-inference-scheduler
kubectl create namespace ${NAMESPACE}

# Create the HuggingFace token secret
kubectl create secret generic llm-d-hf-token \
  --from-literal=HF_TOKEN=<your-hf-token> \
  -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**Note:** Shorter namespace names are recommended to avoid Linux hostname length limitations (64 chars max).

steps.title: Deploy the stack with helmfile
steps.description:
Navigate to the guide directory and deploy:
```bash
cd guides/inference-scheduling
helmfile apply -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**For CPU-only deployment:**
```bash
helmfile apply -e cpu -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**For specific gateway providers:**
```bash
helmfile apply -e kgateway -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**For specific hardware backends:**
```bash
helmfile apply -e amd -n ${NAMESPACE}   # AMD GPUs
helmfile apply -e xpu -n ${NAMESPACE}   # Intel XPU
helmfile apply -e hpu -n ${NAMESPACE}   # Intel Gaudi
helmfile apply -e gke_tpu -n ${NAMESPACE}  # Google TPU
```

steps.title: Install HTTPRoute
steps.description:
Apply the HTTPRoute for your gateway provider:

**For Istio or kgateway:**
```bash
kubectl apply -f httproute.yaml -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**For GKE:**
```bash
kubectl apply -f httproute.gke.yaml -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all 3 Helm releases are deployed:
```bash
helm list -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

Expected releases: `gaie-inference-scheduling`, `infra-inference-scheduling`, `ms-inference-scheduling`.

Verify all pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get all -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

You should see the EPP pod, gateway pod, and 8 model service decode pods (default configuration).

resolution.summary: A successful deployment shows 3 Helm releases (gaie, infra, ms) in deployed status and all pods running. The stack is ready to serve inference requests through the gateway endpoint.

uninstall.title: Remove the HTTPRoute
uninstall.description:
Delete the HTTPRoute for your gateway provider:
```bash
kubectl delete -f httproute.yaml -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

For GKE:
```bash
kubectl delete -f httproute.gke.yaml -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

uninstall.title: Destroy the helmfile deployment
uninstall.description:
Remove all Helm releases using helmfile:
```bash
helmfile destroy -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

Or uninstall manually:
```bash
helm uninstall infra-inference-scheduling -n ${NAMESPACE} --ignore-not-found
helm uninstall gaie-inference-scheduling -n ${NAMESPACE}
helm uninstall ms-inference-scheduling -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

**Note:** If you used `$RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX`, replace release names accordingly: `infra-$RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX`, `gaie-$RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX`, `ms-$RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX`.

uninstall.title: Clean up namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace if no longer needed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace ${NAMESPACE}
```

upgrade.title: Update llm-d version
upgrade.description: Update the llm-d version in your helmfile configuration following the [version selection guide](https://github.com/llm-d/llm-d/tree/main/guides/prereq/client-setup#llm-d-version).

upgrade.title: Apply the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Re-run helmfile apply to upgrade in place:
```bash
helmfile apply -n ${NAMESPACE}
```

Helmfile will perform a diff and apply only the changes.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending due to insufficient GPU resources
troubleshooting.description:
If model service pods are stuck in Pending, your cluster may not have enough GPUs. Check node resources:
```bash
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A 5 'nvidia.com/gpu'
```

Reduce replica count in `ms-inference-scheduling/values.yaml` (e.g., `replicas: 2` for 2 GPUs).

troubleshooting.title: Pod hostname too long causing startup failures
troubleshooting.description:
Long namespace names combined with release names can exceed the 64-character Linux hostname limit. Use a shorter namespace name or set `RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX`:
```bash
RELEASE_NAME_POSTFIX=inf helmfile apply -n llm-d
```

troubleshooting.title: Gateway not routing traffic
Model loading failures
troubleshooting.description:
Check model service pod logs for HuggingFace authentication or download errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/component=modelservice -n ${NAMESPACE} --tail=100
```

Verify the `llm-d-hf-token` secret exists and contains a valid token.

prerequisites.description: A Kubernetes cluster with GPU support (16 GPUs default, configurable), Gateway API CRDs, a gateway provider (Istio/kgateway/GKE), monitoring stack, and a HuggingFace token for model access.
